data.title: Zimmer Biomet raises 2026 adjusted EPS to $8.40-$8.55 while targeting over 200 robotics clinical hires by end of 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Zimmer Biomet (ZBH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We're off to a strong start to the year strategically, operationally and financially" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Ivan Tornos), as the company reported "sales 2.9% on an organic constant currency basis" and "adjusted EPS of $2.09."
  • "U.S. knee growth of 2.2%" included "a greater than 20% increase in partial knee sales" tied to "our Oxford Partial Cementless Knee" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos), while "pressure in our legacy total knee implants such as NexGen and Vanguard" was attributed to "our brand rationalization strategy."
  • "Our technology and data, bone cement and surgical business grew nearly 12%" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos), and he said mBos feedback was "overwhelmingly positive" and that the company "continue[s] to expect U.S. approval and the launch of the semi-autonomous version in early 2027 followed by the fully autonomous version in late 2027 or early 2028."
  • "Dr. Jonathan Vigdorchik... has joined Zimmer Biomet as Chief Science, Technology and Medical Affairs Officer reporting to me" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos), alongside a CFO transition in which "Suky... decision to leave" was announced and "Paul Stellato... will serve as Interim Chief Financial Officer."
  • "Net sales were $2.087 billion" and "we delivered diluted earnings per share of $2.09" (CFO and Executive VP of Finance, Operations & Supply Chain Suketu Upadhyay), with "operating cash flows of $359 million and free cash flow of $246 million" and "$250 million of share repurchases in the first quarter."

Outlook

  • "We are maintaining our full year 2026 organic constant currency revenue growth guidance of 1% to 3%" and "our assumption of up to 100 basis points of price erosion is unchanged" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos).
  • "Our reported sales guidance also remains unchanged at 2.5% to 4.5%" and "we continue to assume up to $750 million of share repurchases" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos).
  • "We are raising both our EPS and free cash flow growth expectations" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos): "adjusted EPS to be $8.40 to $8.55" (from "$8.30 to $8.45" previously) and "free cash flow growth... 9% to 11%" (from "8% to 10%" previously).
  • "We now expect 2026 operating margins to be better than anticipated, down slightly less than 50 basis points from 2025" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos), and he guided cadence including "operating margins in the second quarter of 2026 being down roughly 200 basis points" year-over-year.

Financial Results

  • "We reported GAAP diluted earnings per share of $1.22" and "adjusted gross margin was 73%" (CFO and Executive VP Upadhyay), while "adjusted operating margin was 27.3%."
  • "Consolidated pricing was 40 basis points negative" and results included "opportunistic end-of-quarter purchases above historical levels" (CFO and Executive VP Upadhyay).
  • "Notably, the first quarter saw a $0.20 benefit from tariff-related items" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos), and the CFO added it was "largely driven by the invalidation of the IEEPA tariffs" with "a bit of a pull forward" from refunds.

Q&A

  • Frederick Wise, Stifel: "you didn't raise" revenue guidance despite the quarter; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "the word that comes to mind is confident" and "it's early in the year... This is a year of transition."
  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI: asked for salesforce metrics and macro; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "roughly 66%... were 1099s" at the start of 2026 and "at the end of Q1... slightly below 60%" with turnover "in the single-digit range" and "no material supply disruptions" tied to the Middle East.
  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: asked about one-time items; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "We lost 2 accounts" in U.S. knees and cited "a Kaiser strike" plus "disruption" in moving from "NexGen and Vanguard" to "Persona"; CFO and Executive VP Upadhyay: "we still expect" full-year gross margin "around 71%, give or take."
  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: asked why OUS trailed; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "we made... some distributor changes" and said the company expects "international mid-single digit in the second half of 2026."
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: asked about Monogram rollout and ROSA; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "we continue to anticipate" launch "in early 2027" and said "the combination of Monogram plus ROSA plus TMINI is going to give us a competitive advantage."
  • Matthew Taylor, Jefferies: asked tariff assumptions; CFO and Executive VP Upadhyay: "we are assuming that the 122 tariffs remain intact" and "the IEEPA remain invalidated," while noting "the overall situation remains fluid."
  • Matthew Miksic, Barclays: asked on Paragon momentum and deals; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "we are in the teens" early in Q2 and "we're going to pause" on M&A while "continue to do buybacks."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly skeptical on guidance conservatism and sustainability, pressing on what was "onetime" and why guidance was not lifted more.
  • Management tone was slightly positive to confident in prepared remarks and repeated in Q&A, including "confident" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos) while emphasizing it is "early in the year" and the business is in "transition."
  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on "tempered guidance" and disruption risk, Q1 language shifted to confidence plus specific proof points (salesforce conversion metrics and tariff-related timing).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance changed on profitability and cash but not sales: Q4 guided "adjusted EPS of $8.30 to $8.45" and Q1 raised to "$8.40 to $8.55" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos).
  • The U.S. sales channel narrative moved from explaining "the what, the why, the how and the when" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos, Q4) to reporting progress, including 1099 mix moving "slightly below 60%" and specialization "approaching... 30%."
  • Analysts’ focus stayed concentrated on the sales transition and durability of growth, but Q1 added more scrutiny on one-time tariff and knee-specific disruption drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Modest disruption" from U.S. commercial changes occurred and included "We lost 2 accounts" in U.S. knees (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos).
  • Tariff and policy uncertainty remained in view as "the overall situation remains fluid" (CFO and Executive VP Upadhyay).
  • Management change risk increased with "Suky's decision to leave" and an interim CFO appointment (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos).

Final Takeaway

Zimmer Biomet’s management framed Q1 as a strong start, highlighted measurable progress in converting the U.S. salesforce to more dedicated and specialized coverage, and raised 2026 adjusted EPS and free cash flow growth expectations while keeping the sales growth outlook unchanged. The call also introduced a CFO transition to an interim leader, reaffirmed major technology launch timing for Monogram/mBos, and described knee-specific disruptions and tariff timing effects that influenced quarter results and guidance posture.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Coca-Cola tops Q1 estimates, but PepsiCo still owns the sector's top spot
data.description:
Coca-Cola (KO) shares surged more than 6% on Tuesday after a first-quarter earnings report that beat on nearly every front, yet, despite the strong showing, the beverage giant sits third in Seeking Alpha's Quant Rankings for the soft drinks and non-alcoholic beverages sector, trailing both PepsiCo (PEP) and Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) on a quantitative basis.

PepsiCo leads the sector with a commanding Quant Rating of 4.63, the only Strong Buy on the list, backed by a $210.62B market cap and a solid YTD performance of 7.37%.

Keurig Dr Pepper follows at 3.35, nudging just ahead of Coca-Cola despite being a significantly smaller operation. Both KDP and KO saw modest gains in premarket trading Tuesday, riding the positive sentiment from Coca-Cola's results.

Further down the rankings, Monster Beverage (MNST) sits at 3.13, while Celsius Holdings (CELH) and Primo Brands (PRMB) fall into borderline bearish territory at 2.82 and 2.65 respectively. National Beverage Corp. (FIZZ) is the sole Sell-rated name on the list at 1.93.

For Coca-Cola, the Q1 report, which showed 10% organic revenue growth, 18% comparable EPS growth, and broad-based volume gains across all global segments, makes a compelling case that its Quant Rating could see an upward revision as momentum builds. 

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings grade stocks on their relative performance on various critical quantitative measures, including valuation, growth, stock momentum, and profitability. Ratings are given on a scale from 1 to 5, with any rating of 3.5 or above considered bullish and any rating of 2.5 or below considered bearish.

Here is the list:

  1. PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP), Quant Rating: 4.63

  2. Keurig Dr Pepper Inc. (KDP), Quant Rating: 3.35

  3. The Coca-Cola Company (KO), Quant Rating: 3.28

  4. The Vita Coco Company, Inc. (COCO), Quant Rating: 3.18

  5. Monster Beverage Corporation (MNST), Quant Rating: 3.13

  6. Celsius Holdings, Inc. (CELH), Quant Rating: 2.82

  7. Primo Brands Corporation (PRMB), Quant Rating: 2.65

  8. National Beverage Corp. (FIZZ), Quant Rating: 1.93

Consumer Staples ETFs: (XLP), (VDC), (IYK), (FSTA), (KXI), and (RSPS).

data.title: Applied industrial projects fiscal 2026 EPS of $10.60 to $10.75 as organic sales growth improves
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Applied Industrial Technologies (AIT) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "So overall, we reported a solid third quarter, underpinned by a stronger organic sales growth across the business." (President, CEO & Director Neil Schrimsher)
  • "Specifically, sales increased 6% organically over the prior year, which was the strongest growth in over 2 years. This was up notably from 2% last quarter and at the high end of our third quarter guidance." (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)
  • "Orders, backlog and business funnel activity continue to build positive momentum." (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)
  • "Stronger sales activity was evident across both segments in the quarter with particular strength in our Engineered Solutions segment, which delivered over 9% organic growth year-over-year." (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)
  • "Our exposure to the technology vertical includes an established and ongoing position across the semiconductor space as well as emerging growth opportunities developing within the data center market." (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)
  • "We estimate cross-selling contributed over 100 basis points to the segment's organic growth in the quarter, which is up from the first half fiscal 2026 levels and an encouraging sign." (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)
  • "Year-to-date, we've remained active, deploying over $300 million on share repurchases, M&A and growing our dividend" and "our Board has approved a new authorization to repurchase up to 3 million shares." (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)
  • "Consolidated sales increased 7.3% over the prior year quarter" and "reported earnings per share of $2.65 in the third quarter." (VP, CFO, & Treasurer David Wells)

Outlook

  • "We are tightening our full year fiscal 2026 guidance towards the high end of our prior range" and "we now project EPS within a range of $10.60 to $10.75 based on sales growth of 7.2% to 7.7%, including a 3.8% to 4.2% organic sales growth assumption as well as EBITDA margins of 12.3% to 12.4%." (VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells)
  • "Our updated guidance assumes a fiscal fourth quarter EPS range of $2.85 to $2.96 on organic sales growth of 4% to 5.5% year-over-year as well as EBITDA margins in a range of 12.6% to 12.8%." (VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells)
  • "We remain mindful of ongoing geopolitical developments and trade policy uncertainty, which may continue to influence customer spending behavior." (VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells)

Financial Results

  • "Gross margin of 30.4% was relatively unchanged compared to the prior year level" and "we recognized LIFO expense of $5.6 million compared to $2.2 million in the prior year quarter." (VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells)
  • "SD&A expense as a percentage of sales was at 19.4%, which was relatively unchanged from the prior year, but improved approximately 40 basis points sequentially." (VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells)
  • "Reported EBITDA margin of 12.3% was down 13 basis points from the prior year level" and "results this quarter included $1.7 million or approximately $0.05 per share of nonroutine discrete tax expense related to prior year tax provision adjustments." (VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells)
  • "Cash generated from operating activities during the third quarter was $100.1 million, while free cash flow totaled $95.4 million, representing conversion of approximately 96% relative to net income." (VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells)

Q&A

  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer & Co. Inc., Research Division: "wanted to... go a little deeper into the trends you're seeing with locals versus nationals"; President, CEO & Director Schrimsher: "local accounts year-over-year were up 5% versus 3.5% in Q2" and "national accounts, up 7% year-over-year versus the 4% that we saw in the second quarter."
  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer & Co. Inc., Research Division: "go down a layer or 2 into the flow control"; President, CEO & Director Schrimsher: "they benefited from the tech vertical segment, really 300 basis points" and "Chemicals would be the one that would be still down year-over-year, but the trend is improving on that front."
  • Kenneth Newman, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: "operating leverage... seemed a little bit lighter"; President, CEO & Director Schrimsher: "incrementals in the quarter for ES were 16%, ex LIFO, more 19%" and "within Flow Control, they had some projects that came in lower."
  • Kenneth Newman, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: "timing of those orders flowing through the P&L"; President, CEO & Director Schrimsher: "we want to be prudent" and "there are some that will go in a 60- to 90-day time period. There will be others that can extend out."
  • Andrew Obin, BofA Securities, Research Division: "dig in on M&A environment"; President, CEO & Director Schrimsher: "we are active and engaged at various stages of the M&A process" and "I expect M&A to be a stronger contributor over the next 12 to 18 months."
  • Inara Khan, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division: "how the 6% organic growth this quarter is split between price and volume"; VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells: "pricing in the quarter was about 250 basis points" and "about 350 basis point benefit from volume."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts slightly positive: "real exciting things going on with automation" and "it was really nice to hear about the orders" signaled constructive tone, with targeted probing on margins, M&A timing, and pricing/volume. (Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer; Kenneth Newman, KeyBanc; Andrew Obin, BofA; Inara Khan, Baird)
  • Management slightly positive in prepared remarks, while staying cautious in Q&A: "we remain mindful" (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher) and "we want to be prudent" (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher), alongside confidence signals such as "we are highly confident in our ability to continue to adapt and execute" (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher).
  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on "mixed yet evolving end market backdrop" and "choppy" monthly trends, management’s Q3 language leaned more optimistic on momentum: "orders, backlog and business funnel activity continue to build positive momentum" and "organic sales trending up by a high single-digit percent year-over-year month-to-date in April." (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q3 highlighted a sharper organic re-acceleration: "sales increased 6% organically" and "This was up notably from 2% last quarter" (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher), compared with Q2: "reported year-over-year organic growth of 2.2%" and "growth moderated in December." (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)
  • Q3 guidance language moved tighter and higher on sales growth: "we are tightening our full year fiscal 2026 guidance towards the high end" with sales growth "7.2% to 7.7%" (VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells), versus Q2: "we are adjusting our full year fiscal 2026 EPS guidance" on sales growth "up 5.5% to up 7%." (VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells)
  • Capital returns shifted from Q2’s dividend emphasis ("we announced an 11% increase in our quarterly dividend") to Q3’s repurchase emphasis ("a new authorization to repurchase up to 3 million shares"). (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)

Risks and Concerns

  • "We remain mindful that we continue to operate in a dynamic environment" including "an ongoing dynamic trade policy and tariff backdrop" and "the inflationary environment and suppliers' approach to pricing remains highly fluid at this point." (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)
  • "To date, we have not seen a significant impact from recent tariff and trade policy modifications" and "our teams continue to effectively manage broader inflationary pressures." (President, CEO & Director Schrimsher)
  • "We remain mindful of ongoing geopolitical developments and trade policy uncertainty" and "we continue to assume a degree of variability persists across our end markets near term." (VP, CFO, & Treasurer Wells)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q3 as an inflection in organic growth and demand breadth, citing 6% organic sales growth, stronger Engineered Solutions momentum (including technology vertical exposure spanning semiconductors and data centers), and improving cross-selling contribution in Service Centers. The company tightened full-year fiscal 2026 guidance toward the high end, while pairing caution around geopolitical and trade policy uncertainty with a more upbeat operational narrative on orders, backlog, and early Q4 sales trends, and announced a new authorization to repurchase up to 3 million shares.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Amazon Q1 preview: All eyes on AWS, capital spending
data.description:
When Amazon (AMZN) posts its quarterly results on Wednesday, investors will be closely watching the performance of its cloud division and looking for signs that the tech giant’s heavy investment in artificial intelligence has started to deliver tangible results.

Wall Street expects the e-commerce giant to post EPS of $1.65 on revenue of $177.16B, implying a rise of nearly 14% during the quarter.

Amazon, in February, shocked Wall Street with its annual spending plan of $200 billion, joining its tech peers Google and Meta in the robust spending spree related to boosting AI infrastructure. Investors reacted strongly to this hefty spending plan, leading to the stock tumbling in extended trading after it announced quarterly results.

“The concern here is whether $200B in CapEx burn this year justifies the $15B AWS AI Services run rate. Right now, to me, it doesn't. Therefore, any upgrade in the $200B CapEx guide could lead to a similar reaction post earnings as back in Q4 2025, when the stock was down mid-single digits after the print,” noted a recent Seeking Alpha analysis by Deep Value Investing.

“The full year is where things get a bit trickier. Starting with 2Q guidance, how do investors react to what could be incrementally more negative FCF, and what happens if the Street has allocated too much to operating income after materially raising AWS estimates as we suspect may be the case,” wrote Benchmark analyst Daniel Kurnos.

Investors will also want to get comments on how the higher fuel prices and shipping costs could impact the company.

Despite the concerns, Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street are bullish and rated the stock a Buy and Strong Buy. Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings consider it a Hold.

Top boss Andy Jassy’s letter to shareholders in April shedding light on Amazon’s spending plans and its AI services at its cloud-computing unit helped investors get a near-term picture.

Three years into this AI wave, AWS' AI revenue run rate is over $15B in the first quarter of 2026 (nearly 260 times larger than AWS at that same point), and ascending rapidly, Jassy said, adding that the company has very high demand and customers want AWS for core and AI workloads, and "we're monetizing capacity as fast as we can install it."

“While we forecast '26/'27 AWS revenue +29%/+30%, we see potential upside to +42%/+44% if capex is deployed in linear fashion and revenue/GW holds at historical levels,” said Oppenheimer analyst Jason Helfstein. However, he added that advertising trends remain healthy, but it is expected to slow vs. FY25.

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives said he expects another “strong beat as the Street continues to underestimate how rapidly AWS is reaccelerating into a supply-constrained AI demand backdrop.”

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen four upward revisions compared to 22 downward revisions, while revenue estimates have been revised upwards 30 times versus two downward moves.

The stock has risen over 13% so far this year, outperforming the nearly 5% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

data.title: Coca-Cola Europacific Partners impresses Jefferies with its Q1 earnings report
data.description:
Jefferies reiterated its bullish view on Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (CCEP) after pointing to the solid start of the year displayed in the company's Q1 earnings report.

With its Q1 earnings report. Coca-Cola Europacific Partners reported revenue was up 6.7% on a reported basis to €5.0B, driven mainly by strong growth in Europe and continued momentum in zero-sugar and energy categories. Underlying demand was solid, with reported volume up 8.5%, but average daily sales volume was up a more modest 1.6% after adjusting for six extra trading days, while revenue per unit case increased 0.8% as pricing and mix continued to support the top line.  

Analyst Edward Mundy and his team view Coca-Cola Europacific (CCEP) as the “real thing” in the consumer staples sector, noting it is a well-run, consistent compounder with high barriers to entry that can deliver visible double-digit returns in a low-growth environment. Mundy noted the firm still has conviction in its forecast for 4% sales growth and 7% EBIT growth over the medium term with cash returns to shareholders. 

Within the Coke system, Coca-Cola Europacific (CCEP) is one of Jefferies' top picks. "Changes over the past decade in incidence and scale have made the system more robust and aligned; a step change in bottler execution capabilities and improving customer relationships provide further resilience," highlighted Mundy.

Jefferies has a Buy rating on CCEP.

data.title: Sherwin-Williams keeps 2026 guidance unchanged while raising raw material inflation outlook to low-to-mid single digits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sherwin-Williams (SHW) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Sherwin-Williams said it delivered “strong sales in a quarter characterized by heightened global uncertainty and persistent demand softness in most end markets,” while noting “sales exceeded guidance on a consolidated basis and in all 3 reportable segments” (Senior Vice President James Jaye).
  • CEO Heidi Petz framed the quarter as continued share capture, saying, “Our differentiation continues to widen the gap between Sherwin-Williams and our competitors as evidenced by our strong top line and robust new account growth across the business” (Chairman, CEO & President Heidi Petz).
  • In Paint Stores Group, Petz said PSG “grew by a mid-single-digit percentage,” with “Price/mix and volume both increased by low single-digit percentages,” and “Effectiveness of our January 1 price increase is trending slightly better than expected” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Petz highlighted specific end-market trends: “residential repaint… returned to mid-single-digit growth,” “property maintenance was encouraging,” and “demand in new residential remained very challenging as we anticipated,” while “Protective & Marine… sales increased by double digits” and marked “the seventh straight quarter of high single-digit growth” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • In Consumer Brands, Petz said results were “driven by high-teens growth from the Suvinil acquisition,” with “Softness… in North America where sales decreased by low single digits,” while “Group sales, excluding Suvinil increased by low single digits” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • In Performance Coatings, Petz said sales growth was supported by “growth in every division and region,” while noting the demand backdrop: “demand in our underlying core business is still declining in some end markets” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • CFO Ben Meisenzahl pointed to the pricing shift as the year evolved, saying, “we have more than twice the pricing now in this new guide than what we had in the original guidance that we gave you in January,” and described the approach as “balancing price with the right volume” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Benjamin Meisenzahl).

Outlook

  • Petz said, “Our consolidated sales and earnings guidance for the full year are unchanged,” while adding that “the Middle East conflict… has added further complexity and uncertainty in navigating the macro landscape” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Petz introduced a more cautious demand view: “we expect to see some negative impacts on demand from recent events as the year progresses,” and reiterated the stance on end markets: “little support for meaningful recovery in most end markets” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Petz increased the inflation view for inputs, saying, “we… increase our full year raw material inflation outlook to the range of up, low to mid-single digits,” and tied timing to the P&L: “sustained inflation… typically takes about a quarter or 2 before we begin seeing an impact” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Petz said pricing expectations rose: “our expectation for consolidated price/mix for the year increases to the high end of our low single-digit range,” adding, “we are prepared to implement additional increases, if necessary” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).

Financial Results

  • Jaye reported “Consolidated sales grew by a high single-digit percentage,” and “Reported gross margin expanded by 90 basis points,” noting it was “inclusive of a dilutive impact from Suvinil” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Jaye said “Adjusted diluted net income per share… increased by a mid-single-digit percentage,” and “adjusted EBITDA increased by a high single-digit percentage” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Jaye highlighted cash and leverage, stating “Net operating cash improved by $200 million,” and “We ended the first quarter… a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 2.5x” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Jaye said Sherwin-Williams returned capital with “$773 million to shareholders through share buybacks and dividends” (Senior Vice President Jaye).

Q&A

  • John McNulty, BMO: asked why pricing sounded “more surgical”; CFO Meisenzahl said pricing in the updated guide is “more than twice” January’s, with regional and end-market phasing and “plenty of time… to get additional pricing” if needed (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Patrick Fischer, Goldman Sachs: asked what drove higher raw material outlook; Jaye cited “solvents and resins,” said “Propylene drives about 75% of our basket,” and referenced a forecast that propylene “maybe up 50% more through the rest of ’26” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: asked about removing “select commodity inflation”; Jaye said it was removed because “it’s very obvious that the commodities are moving upward” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Christopher Parkinson, Wolfe: asked about growth spend and store strategy into 2027/2028; Petz said the company will be “extremely aggressive” in a “jump ball environment,” and reiterated a “disciplined process of looking at our portfolio” focused on “return on net assets employed” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird: asked about volume shifting to “low single-digit decline” in guidance mechanics; CFO Meisenzahl tied softer volume to inflation/demand impact and cited “the lowest level on record” consumer sentiment prints (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • John Roberts, Mizuho: asked about housing affordability actions; CFO Meisenzahl said the company wants “more of the supply opportunities” and is watching for land/supply steps that lower builder costs (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley: asked about Consumer Brands margin and reallocations; CFO Meisenzahl said improvement came from “global supply chain efficiencies” and “no reallocation… Nothing here” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Michael Harrison, Seaport: asked if the “7%” increase conversations were complete and about mid-season moves; Petz said “all of those conversations have happened,” and “if we need to go again, we will go again” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Gregory Melich, Evercore: asked about gross margin and Suvinil dilution; CFO Meisenzahl said “we would have been over 100 basis point improvement without Suvinil” and warned margin can be “a little bit lumpy” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Arun Viswanathan, RBC: asked if volume strength continues; Petz said, “The answer is absolutely, we see those continuing,” and cited “Emerald Symmetry” as “our best-performing interior product that we’ve ever produced” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research: asked what drove higher pricing and raw material cadence; CFO Meisenzahl said pricing included “everything… in January… [and] everything new… since then,” with raw inflation “heavier in the second half” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Joshua Spector, UBS: challenged why raw inflation isn’t higher; CFO Meisenzahl cited supplier relationships, saying procurement is “tighter than some of the others,” and pointed to “50% of our business on contracts” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Matthew DeYoe, BofA: asked how much packaging growth is tied to regulation; Petz said the EFSA BPA ban “is scheduled to take effect in Q2 of this year” and should drive conversions “into ’27” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Charles Cerankosky, Northcoast: asked mortgages/household formations; Jaye said mortgage rates are “not expecting it to move a whole lot,” and referenced “a tremendous pent-up demand” from underbuilding (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Patrick Cunningham, Citi: asked about PCG volume guide and order timing; CFO Meisenzahl cited supply timing (“boats… 60 to 90 days”) and a potential “inflection point” on feedstocks affecting inflation/demand (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Kevin Estok, Jefferies: asked about customer ordering behavior; Petz said “it’s probably a mix” between prudence and “confidence in the backlogs” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Zack Pacheco, Loop: asked about pre-buying; Petz said, “We’re not seeing that in this moment, nothing material” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Michael Sison, Wells Fargo: asked if architectural demand is “structurally impaired” and PSG guide; Petz said “I wouldn’t use the word impaired… under pressure,” and Jaye said PSG full-year guidance “remains in line in that low single digit” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Sebastian Bray, Berenberg: asked about refinish and EMEA decorative; Petz tied refinish gains to combining “controlled distribution” and “waterborne technologies,” while CFO Meisenzahl said Europe had a “reporting mix impact” and core Europe growth was “mid-single-digit” excluding reclass (Chairman, CEO & President Petz; Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Eric Bosshard, Cleveland Research: asked about DIY divergence; Petz said the premium DIY customer “generally prefers the specialty store environment,” while value DIY relies on retail partnerships (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan: asked about industrial price magnitude and propylene math; CFO Meisenzahl cited “bigger… on industrial, in APAC… EMEA,” and Jaye said the 50% figure was a “forecast,” adding “we’re not buying propylene… [but] derivatives” that “take some time to flow into our basket” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl; Senior Vice President Jaye).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to pressing, with repeated challenges on inflation math and pricing adequacy, including “we have math out there that says you could see raws up 20%” and “wouldn’t that mean that your raw materials are up 38%, 37%” (Joshua Spector, UBS; Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan).
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive and confident, emphasizing agility and readiness to reprice: “we are prepared to implement additional increases, if necessary,” and “if we need to go again, we will go again” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Versus last quarter, management kept the “share gain” and “little support for… recovery” framing, but the catalyst shifted from tariffs/soft demand to geopolitical-driven volatility, with Petz saying “What has changed is the Middle East conflict” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current-quarter guidance language kept the full-year view steady (“unchanged”), but the input-cost framing shifted from Q4’s “raw material basket… up a low single-digit percentage… driven by tariffs along with select commodities” to Q1’s “increase… to the range of up, low to mid-single digits” tied to Middle East-driven energy/petrochemical volatility (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Q1 commentary leaned more into targeted pricing execution and phasing, with CFO Meisenzahl describing region/end-market timing, while Q4 emphasized a broad “7% price increase effective January 1” with “Realization… in the low single-digit range” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl; Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Prepared remarks maintained the share-gain posture, but Q1 Q&A featured more direct analyst skepticism on raw inflation magnitude and competitor actions, leading management to reiterate procurement advantages and contract coverage (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).

Risks and Concerns

  • Petz said geopolitical uncertainty is expected to pressure demand: “we expect to see some negative impacts on demand… as the year progresses,” and warned the situation is “highly fluid” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Petz flagged commodity volatility, stating “cost for oil, natural gas and key petrochemical feedstocks… have inflated and remain volatile,” with P&L timing more pronounced “through the second quarter and into the second half of the year” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Management emphasized supply assurance as mitigation, with Petz saying the “first objective is certainty of supply,” and highlighting that “over 80% of our consolidated revenue is in North America” with raws “sourced in region” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).

Final Takeaway

Sherwin-Williams management characterized Q1 as a share-gain quarter with high single-digit consolidated sales growth and continued gross margin expansion, while keeping full-year sales and earnings guidance unchanged. The key call shift was a higher raw material inflation outlook to low-to-mid single digits amid Middle East-driven volatility, paired with an expectation for price/mix at the high end of a low single-digit range and a stated willingness to take additional targeted pricing if needed, while prioritizing supply continuity and volume opportunities across segments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Honeywell rebounds after six straight declines
data.description:
Shares of Honeywell International (HON) rose 0.19% in the afternoon trade on Tuesday to $212.46, ending a six-session losing streak.

The stock had fallen around 7.7% between April 20 and April 27, while the S&P 500, rose 0.91% over the same period.

Shares of the U.S. industrial giant have come under pressure in recent sessions amid a mix of corporate developments and earnings-related headlines. The company reported mixed first-quarter results, with an earnings beat offset by a revenue miss and a weaker-than-expected second-quarter outlook tied to Middle East-related disruptions.

At the same time, Honeywell continued to reshape its portfolio, announcing the $1.4 billion sale of its productivity solutions unit and progress on additional divestitures, alongside developments such as the confidential IPO filing of its quantum computing unit Quantinuum. 

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system, HON is rated Hold with a score of 3.11 out of 5, with a A in profitability, but offset by weaker grades of D in terms of growth and a D+ in valuation.

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis said Honeywell is continuing to streamline its operations through the divestiture of its PSS business. The analyst pointed out that “I believe its divestiture further simplifies HON’s portfolio and sharpens its focus around higher-margin, less cyclical automation and software-driven businesses.”

Turning to the Wall Street, analysts are bullish, with 15 out of 26 analysts rate the stock with a buy or higher, 11 suggest to hold and two of them rate it with a Sell or lower.

Shares have fallen about 5.2% over the past month but are up 8.5% year to date.

data.title: First Financial Bankshares raises dividend by 15.8% to $0.22
data.description:
• First Financial Bankshares (FFIN) declares $0.22/share quarterly dividend, 15.8% increase from prior dividend of $0.19.
  • Forward yield 2.69%
  • Payable July 1; for shareholders of record June 12; ex-div June 12.
  • See FFIN Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.
